title: What’s on my minimalist iPhone 17 Pro!
transcript: This video isn't going to be a regular what's on my iPhone. I'm going to show you how I organized my super minimal iPhone setup, how I've been using it lately, and why I decided to completely change the way I use my phone. And by the way, everything I did is totally free. You can do it on your own phone, too, without downloading or paying for anything. title: iPhone 17 Pro Max 1 Month Later There Are Some Issues !!
transcript: So, I've been using the iPhone 17 Pro Max for a while now, and being honest, this iPhone is great, but it has a few small issues. First up, I went with the silver color. I know a lot of people are into that shiny, vibrant orange color, but it wasn't for me. I went for a much more muted and timeless color. It looks great even with a case, and the most important part is that it is a lot less prone to scratches and scuffs. This year, Apple used aluminum instead of titanium on their Pro models. Aluminum definitely helps with heat dissipation, but at the end of the day, it scratches a lot easier, and if you don't use a case, it can be a nightmare to keep this phone in pristine condition. I'm using this clear case for my iPhone 17 Pro Max, and it does the job pretty well. I have a few other cases, too, from Pitaka. They go really well with my silver iPhone 17 Pro Max. Orange iPhones are already turning pink, so there's definitely some sort of durability issue with this year's iPhone model. Anyway, the rest of the phone has been great. The only issue I faced was random Wi-Fi disconnection. I'm using the 512 GB model, but it randomly disconnects from my home Wi-Fi network. This issue isn't there on any of my other Apple devices. I checked out the internet and it seems the issue is widely spread. I updated to iOS 26.0.1, but the issue was still there. And I even upgraded to iOS 26.1 beta 4 and this random disconnection issue is still there. I don't know if this is a hardware issue or a software glitch that is affecting my unit. Apart from that, I'm facing no issues at all. The performance has been great and the cameras are superb. The telephoto camera, to be precise, is also doing great. There's not a massive difference in quality and performance when comparing it with the iPhone 16 Pro Max, but now the image is cleaner and noise-free at higher zoom levels. The PWM dimming technology is helping me, too. I've been getting less eye strain lately after turning on this feature. If you have an iPhone 17 model, I'd highly recommend turning on this option if you get a lot of eye strain. I'm using a screen protector, so I wasn't able to test the new anti-reflective coating. Some people on the internet are saying not to use a screen protector at all, as it can ruin the anti-reflective coating. But for me, the safety of the display comes first, which is why I'm using this borderless screen protector. The display this year is a lot brighter and now it goes up to 3000 nits. Although I use my iPhone mostly indoors, so I'm not really benefiting from that. Sound quality is about the same as the iPhone 16 Pro. I haven't noticed any drastic difference in sound quality. Moving on to battery life, the most crucial part for a lot of people. Battery life on this iPhone is just crazy good. Many tests like from Phone Buff already show the winner is clearly the iPhone 17 Pro Max, beating the Galaxy S25 Ultra from Samsung. It's easily a 2-day battery life smartphone for me. I charge it daily and I have kept the battery charging limit to 80% as it helps preserve the battery health. Overall, the iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max are solid phones from Apple this year. There are a few issues like the scratch gate and Wi-Fi issues, but other than that, this phone is phenomenal. If you have an iPhone 15 Pro or older, I'd suggest trying out this phone for once, and you'll definitely like it a lot. If you're using an iPhone 17 or 17 Pro, then let me know in the comments how your experience has been so far. And with that said, I'll catch you in the next one.

title: iPhone 17 & 17 Pro (Max) Camera & Photo Settings Tutorial
transcript: There are many useful features hidden behind the  simple interface of the iPhone Camera app. Today,   I'll show you everything you need to know to get  the best possible results with your iPhone 17,   17 Air, and of course the 17 Pro and Pro  Max. I Will focus on the photo settings.   For the video settings there will be  another video. For a better overview,   the video is divided into 20 parts corresponding  to the most important camera features. The chapter   titles allow you to quickly find specific parts. And there's something else I want to show you   today. You'll see that for the Night Mode  or even when using the new selfie camera,   it can be extremely useful to be able to place  your iPhone in a stable position. That's why   I looked for a simple solution to quickly and  securely place my iPhone in an upright position.   And I found a great solution for the iPhone  17 Pro – which I'll show you a little later. You can open the camera app in different ways:  Of course by tapping the app icon, in the control   center you will find it in the lower right corner,  from the lock screen by swiping to the left or by   pressing the camera icon, and of course by  pressing the camera control button. But this   button can do much more than just open the camera  - I'll show you all its features a little later. Let's start simple, with the  basic operation of the app.  When you start the camera app, you  will see the shutter button here,   a small preview of your last shot at  the bottom left, and the small icon   on the right allows you to switch to the  front camera, which has received a number   of new features for the iPhone 17, which  we will also take a closer look at later. Here in the middle, you can switch  between shooting modes. Initially,   you will only see the photo and video  modes, but if you swipe left or right,   the other shooting modes will also appear. On the right there are the four photo modes Photo,   Portrait, Spatial and Pano. on the left you  find the four video modes Video, Cinematic,   Slo-Mo and Time-Lapse. While photo and video are  self-explanatory, you can use Portrait to create   a blurred background. Although this actually also  works with objects, the mode is called Portrait,   because this effect is especially well suited  for portraits. With Spatial you can not only   take photos but also videos with a spatial 3D  effect and then play them back on the Vision Pro   or the Meta Quest headsets. If you do not have  a VR Headset, this mode is currently useless.  In Panorama mode you can capture images  with a field of view that is much wider   than the camera lens would actually allow. This  is especially suitable for landscape photography,   for example. The Cinematic Mode is a kind of  Portrait Mode for video. And with the slow-motion   mode and the timelapse mode, you can take cool  slow-motion, time-lapse, or hyperlapse shots.  But as I said, today we are  talking about the photo modes. Let's first have a look at the different  functions of the shutter button. If you   want to take a simple photo, of course  you tap the shutter button briefly. You   can achieve the same result by using the  volume buttons. It makes no difference   which of the two buttons you use. If you want  to quickly capture several photos in a row,   you can swipe the shutter button to the left. Now  your Iphone will capture photos in an extremely   fast sequence until you lift your finger. This  is called the Burst Mode. This feature is often   used for photographing action. But it can also  help when taking portraits, for example to avoid   closed eyes. You simply take several photos in  quick succession and then select the best one.  By the way, in the settings you can also set the  Burst Mode to be activated by using the Volume Up   button. This would be especially interesting  if you want to use this mode frequently. If you press and hold the Shutter button,  the Iphone automatically starts to capture   a video. The recording ends as soon as you  release the Shutter button. This way you   can quickly capture a video without having  to switch to video mode first. Apple calls   this QuickTake. If you want to capture a longer  video then drag the shutter right onto the lock.   Now the Iphone will continue recording even if  you release the shutter button. While recording   you can also continue to take pictures by  tapping on the shutter button that appears   on the right. And here on the left you can  pause and resume the recording. You can also   start capturing a QuickTake video by pressing  and holding one of the two volume buttons. Now let's take a look at the zoom and the lenses. Above the menu bar you can see a small 1x. It   is yellow. This means that the main camera is  currently active. By tapping on the 0.5 to the   left of it, you activate the ultra-wide camera  and via the 4 the camera with the telephoto   lens. However, the latter is only available  on the two Pro models. The 2 and the 8 do not   stand for additional cameras. Rather, only a  smaller part of the sensor is used for taking   the picture. Nevertheless, the high-resolution  48-megapixel sensors enable high-quality results.  And the high-resolution sensor of the main  camera also makes another feature possible:   if you tap on the 1, you can activate  a focal length of 28mm, that is 1.2x,   and 35mm, that is 1.5x. Yes, this is also a  digital crop, but similar to the case of the   2x zoom, there is hardly any loss of quality  compared to the native focal length of 1x,   certainly also because the images  are digitally processed again.  The default field of view of the main camera is  relatively wide at 24mm, or 1x. If you prefer that   the main camera is set to 1.2x or 1.5x by default  when you start the camera app, you can set that in   the settings of the camera app under FusionCamera  and Default Lens. This is a useful option because,   for example, 35mm, which is 1.5x, is considered  the most flexible focal length to use.  When choosing a lens, one thing should  be clear to you. Even if it does not   look like this on the display, the different  lenses have different technical properties.  The main camera produces less distortions  than the ultra-wide camera and it has the   largest aperture. It can therefore achieve better  results in low light conditions. The telephoto   lens produces the least amount of distortion and  is therefore best suited for portraits. However,   it has the smallest aperture and therefore  achieves the worst results in low light. If you now tap and hold one of the numbers, a kind  of zoom slider appears and you can swipe left and   right to zoom in and out more precisely.  For this tutorial I use an iPhone 17 Pro,   so you can see 0.5, 1,2, 4, 7 and 49 as numbers.  The iPhone 17 and 17 Air have only a 10x digital   zoom range.These numbers of course stand for  the different cameras of your iPhone. If the   quality of your photos is important to you,  you should avoid a zoom factor between the   numbers. And you should also avoid a zoom  factor above 8, because in all these cases   it results in a digital crop of the image. You can also zoom in and out by using two   fingers. In this case you can always  read the current zoom factor here. When you capture a picture with your Iphone,  you will of course determine the framing on   the display. So what should be on the picture,  how should the picture composition look like.   Basically the framing of your picture  will correspond to this outer frame. In   many cases you can also see the area outside  this frame.This exceeding part is slightly   darkened. It is supposed to help you to find  the right framing, but it is not captured. For the right composition the grid also helps  you. So you can stick to the Rule of Thirds,   for example, which is important for a good  composition. And this white line is supposed   to help you keep your iPhone straight when  taking a photo and avoid a crooked horizon.   It appears when you rotate your iPhone. As soon  as the line turns yellow, you can be sure that   the horizon is straight. If the grid or  the level do not appear on your iPhone,   you can activate them in the settings  of the camera app under Composition. In addition to the right framing, focus  and exposure are crucial for a good photo.   The iPhone's camera sets focus and exposure  automatically and does an excellent job. Faces   are recognized perfectly. You can see this by the  yellow frame that appears when the camera detects   a face. And actually, with the iphone you can also  get a blurred background or foreground naturally,   for example when you capture objects in the near  foreground and the background is far away. So   it is quite possible that certain areas of the  image are in focus and others are not. Therefore   it can sometimes be necessary to adjust the  focus manually. You do this by tapping on   the object or person in the image that should  be in focus. Now your iPhone also adjusts the   exposure to the new focus. You can also correct  the exposure manually. You do this by tapping on   the sun next to the focus square and by swiping  up or down. Upwards the image becomes brighter,   downwards it becomes darker. You can also lock  the focus and the exposure. Suppose you set the   focus and/or the exposure manually and want them  to stay locked even when you move the phone. You   can do this by tapping and holding on the  appropriate area. Now the AE/AF Lock sign   appears in yellow at the top. You can now move  your phone without changing focus and exposure.   This can be useful for example if you want to  change the framing after setting the focus. The ultra-wide camera of the iPhone 17 & 17 Pro  has a very special feature. It can focus extremely   close. Apple calls this Macro Mode, because by  getting very close, you can capture small subjects   greatly magnified. You don't need to have the  ultra-wide camera activated for this. The iPhone   automatically switches to Macro Mode when the  distance to an object becomes less than 14 cm.   So if the ultra-wide camera is not active, the  iPhone automatically switches from the main camera   or the telephoto lens to the ultra-wide camera.  You will notice this when there are strange   image jumps. This happens automatically. If you  want to have more control over the Macro Mode,   you can activate the Macro Control feature in  the settings of the camera app. Now, whenever   the Macro Mode is active, a yellow flower appears  at the bottom left. By tapping on the flower,   you can now also deactivate the Macro Mode. Personally, I think that the macro mode is   a great feature. You should try  it out - the results are amazing. Apart from these basic features there are  a few additional settings and features. To   use them you have to open an additional  menu. You can do this by tapping on the   icon with the 6 small dots in the top right  corner or by swiping up with your finger to   the left or right of the shutter button. An  additional menu will now appear where you can   activate or deactivate certain features.  On the top left you will find the flash.   You can set it to automatic, on or off. Notice how  the flash symbol at the top of the screen changes. The icon for the night mode only appears  at the bottom when it is dark. The Night   Mode will dramatically change the results in  low light conditions. Normally the Night Mode   will activate automatically when it is very dark.  When the night mode icon at the top turns yellow,   you know that the night mode is active. In  NightMode your IPhone uses longer exposure times.   Depending on how dark it is, your iPhone will  need a shorter or longer exposure time to get   a good result. You can see the duration of the  automatically set exposure time right next to   the icon, for example 1 or 2 seconds. During the  exposure you should hold your iPhone as still as   possible. At the top of the preview image a timer  appears which counts down the remaining time.   If you shake or move the iPhone, the device will  recognize this and two crosshairs will appear,   one yellow and one white. You should move the  iPhone in a way that the yellow crosshair lies   directly on top of the white one. This helps to  reduce movements and you can get a better result.  By tapping the Night Mode icon, you can disable  the Night Mode or activate Night Mode Max. Night   Mode Max can be extremely useful. With Night Mode  Max, the exposure time is extended even further.   This doesn't make the image brighter, but if you  have set up your iPhone so that it is stable and   thus eliminate camera shake, you can achieve the  best possible results in low light conditions   with a longer exposure time and Night Mode Max. And since the best option for setting up your   iPhone so that it is stable is always the one  you have with you, I looked for a high-quality   case with an integrated stand for my iPhone 17  Pro. And I'm very happy to have discovered the   new MagicStand case from Casekoo. Casekoo has  also agreed to sponsor this video. The Magic   Stand is the first stand that can be rotated a  full 360 degrees and is permanently integrated   into the case. This allows you to position the  iPhone vertically or horizontally. In many cases,   this means you no longer need a tripod to film  yourself. You can also use the Magic Stand to   read a book, watch a movie, or—as I often do  myself—use your iPhone for video conferences.   This case from Casekoo, das es natürlich auch für  das iPhone 16 oder 15 gibt, is available in all   kinds of colors, of course. While I often  prefer simple colors like this deep blue,   I have to admit, the cosmic orange  color looks very cool and would of   course go really well with the orange iPhone  17 Pro. And the case has another advantage:   the camera cutout has a relatively high edge,  which means the lenses are very well protected.   You can also use the stand to hold your iPhone  securely so that you don't drop it when taking   selfies. If you like the case, use the link  in the description for an additional discount. The icon at the top center refers to Live Photos,  one of my favorite iphone photo features. Here,   too, the automatic is normally active. You can  also turn LivePhotos on or off permanently here.  With LivePhotos your iphone captures a clip  of one and a half seconds before and after   the photo was taken. Therefore a short video clip  of three seconds is created. You can watch this   miniclip by tapping and holding on the screen  when viewing your photo. You can change the   so-called Key Photo within your clip and you  can also add cool effects to your Live Photo.   It's very easy to do. To add effects, you open  your Live Photo and tap on the Live icon in the   upper left corner. You can add a loop to repeat  your shot continuously or bounce to repeat your   shot alternately forwards and backwards. I  find Long Exposure especially interesting.   This simulates a long exposure. It works well  with moving lights or for example with water.   If you tap on the settings icon at the bottom and  then on the Live icon you can also change the key   photo and shorten the duration of the clip. It  can be extremely useful to change the key photo,   for example if your subject has closed his or  her eyes in the previous key photo. We will take   a look at the other editing features later. You can tell that your iphone is capturing   a Live Photo by the yellow Live  icon at the top of the screen.  The next icon is a timer. This allows you to set  that the image is not taken until three , five or   ten seconds after pressing the shutter button.  This can be useful if you want to take a group   selfie and place your iPhone on a tripod or in low  light with longer exposures to avoid any shaking. On the left, you will find a very important  feature, the Exposure Slider. With this feature   you can make your image brighter or darker, that  means you can overexpose or underexpose your image   a little bit in relation to the settings of the  automatic exposure control and this for a maximum   of 2 stops. But wait, wasn't there already the sun  icon for that? Yes, but with the exposure slider   you can make smaller and more precise adjustments  and you have a little more control. In addition,   the exposure correction will be kept and will  stay the same if you take more photos. I have   to say that the automatic of the iPhone protects  so-called highlights very well and there are   hardly any overexposed areas in the pictures.  So for such cases you hardly need any exposure   correction. It's more that you might want to  capture a darker or brighter image to create   a certain mood. For example, many people believe  that the iPhone exposes the images too brightly   and prefer a slightly negative value here. But  beware, if you no longer need this function,   you should deactivate it again. Otherwise, all  your pictures will be under- or overexposed. This icon stands for the photographic styles. The  Photographic Styles are not to be confused with   filters. In its default setting, the iPhone  produces a relatively natural look that is   relatively close to reality. Other smartphone  manufacturers, for example, prefer a more vivid   look with more saturation or more contrast. With  the Photographic Styles you can now change the   general look of your iPhone photos. Unlike  a filter, it does not simply apply a color   to the whole image. Photographic Styles adjust  specific colors in certain parts of your photos   to create the exact look you want. And unlike  with older iPhone models, the iPhone 17 allows   you to change and customise the photographic  style even after the photo has been taken. There   are basically two types of styles: undertones,  which mainly influence the undertone of the skin,   and mood styles, which give the whole image  a special look. Undertones include Cool Rose,   Neutral, Bright, Rose Gold, gold or Amber, while  under Mood you will find for example Vibrant or   Dramatic, Cosy or Stark Black & White for a very  high-contrast black and white photo. You can use   the slider at the bottom to adjust the intensity  of the effect, whereby the effect is neutralised,   at least as far as the colour is concerned, if  you drag the slider all the way to the left.   You can then make further adjustments using  the field in the centre. To put it simply,   the further you drag the slider to the right, the  more saturated the effect becomes; if you drag   it upwards, parts of the image are brightened and  the contrast is reduced; if you drag it downwards,   the image appears darker and the contrast  is increased. One thing should be clear:   The style remains active even when you close the  app. So if some of your photos look a bit strange,   this could be due to an unintentionally active  photographic style. And Apple has come up with   a guide to help you find your ideal style and  set it as the new standard. When you open the   settings in the Camera app, under Photographic  Styles you can select 4 photos , ideally photos   that have different subjects, and can now  see at a glance what effects the respective   photographic style has on your photos. You can  then select your favourite style, adjust it if   necessary and save it as the new default. This  allows you to define your own personal style for   your iPhone photos. However, only the undertones  are available here in the settings. And as I said,   if you don't like a photo with a certain style,  you can now, in contrast to older iPhones,   open the photo and adjust the photographic  style later via the settings and styles. With the icon to the right of the Photographic  Styles you can change the aspect ratio of your   photos. A normal photo sensor delivers a  photo in a 4:3 format. You can also set   a square aspect ratio or a 16:9 aspect ratio  which is more typical for video recordings. You   should not forget that you can also change  the aspect ratio when editing your photos. Before we take a look at the portrait mode, let's  take a closer look at the new selfie camera,   the so-called Center Stage Camera of the iPhone  17. The selfie camera also supports features such   as the Night Mode and the Live Photo Mode. And you  can zoom in and out using the two small arrows,   whereby this zoom effect is significantly bigger  than on older iPhones. But what's really cool is   that you can use this icon here to switch from  portrait to landscape format without turning the   iPhone. Why would that be useful? For example,  if you want to take a group selfie, you no   longer have to hold your iPhone sideways, which  was not only awkward but also rarely produced   good results. This new feature is possible because  the selfie camera's sensor is now square. And the   CenterStage feature, which you can activate via  the icon at the top, can also set the optimal zoom   factor or format automatically when you activate  Autozoom or Autorotate here. The camera will now   automatically zoom out or rotate the image if  not everyone of the group fits in the frame.  It is also interesting that the Selfie-camera  shows you a mirrored image in the preview,   but captures the image without mirroring it. The  picture then looks different from the preview.   If this bothers you, you can change this  in the settings. When capturing selfies,   remember that you can use the volume buttons  as shutter. This is especially useful here. If we now switch to portrait mode, we find  the usual settings for flash, timer, exposure,   styles and aspect ratio in the menu. LivePhoto is  not available. However, there are two new settings   here, different portrait lighting effects, which  you can select here by swiping to the left and   right and the F-Stop or Aperture. As explained  in the beginning, in portrait mode your iPhone   creates a blurred background, as if you had taken  the picture with a Mirrorless or DSLR and a fast   lens. In general, the smaller the number of  the F-stop the stronger the effect will be,   that means the blurrier the background will  be. The bigger the number the smaller the   effect will be. Just like it would be on a  bigger camera. Basically the feature works   incredibly well. Only in very small details,  such as protruding hairs, can you sometimes   spot imperfections. As already mentioned the  portrait mode also works with objects. But   especially with objects you have to make sure  that there is an appropriate distance between   camera and object to activate the feature. The  camera tells you this at the top of the screen. And Portrait Lighting is also a great feature.  It is designed to simulate professional lighting   techniques. If you've been into photography,  you'll know that good lighting often makes   a bigger difference than the actual camera.  You can select different lighting effects by   swiping to the left and right. You can also  see the effect in the preview. With Studio   Light the face is well exposed by a soft  light. It reminds you of a beauty filter,   with Contour Light the external areas of the  face or the object are darkened a bit. This   gives the picture a little more depth and drama.  With Stage Light the subject is isolated in the   spotlight. The outer area is completely darkened.  Stage Light Mono creates the same effect in black   and white and High-Key Light Mono does not darken  but lightens the outer area, for example like a   portrait against a white background. If you find  the effect exaggerated or too weak, you can adjust   the intensity. In the menu tap on the Intensity  icon and now you can adjust the effect by dragging   the slider to the left or right. Also note that  you can change and adjust the Portrait Lighting   and F-Stop when editing your portrait. And not  only that - when you edit your portrait, you can   even move the focus point using the yellow frame. Here on the left, you can also switch between   the main camera, the 2x Crop and the telephoto  lens on the 17 Pro and Pro Max. For best results   of close-ups of faces, I recommend using  the telephoto lens. And there's something   else you should know. If you are in the normal  photo mode and your iPhone recognizes a face,   an F icon appears at the bottom left. This means  you can activate the portrait feature in normal   photo mode with a simple tap and don't have  to switch to portrait mode. And you can even   activate the portrait feature when editing the  photo - it's only important that your iPhone has   detected a face when taking the picture. By  the way, this also works with dogs and cats. With the Panorama mode you can capture an  extremely wide field of view. As the name   suggests, this is great for capturing  landscapes or cityscapes. Basically,   several images are combined. To do this, start  recording and move your camera along the arrow   from left to right. If you tap on the arrow you  can also change the direction and capture the   panorama from right to left. Make sure that  the arrow is moving along the yellow line   during the recording. If you do not succeed,  you will see unsightly waves in the image.   Apart from that there are hardly any settings in  this mode. You can change the lens and that's it. Now that we've learnt about most of the features  of the camera app, it's time to take a closer look   at the camera control button. And the button has  some very interesting features. As mentioned at   the beginning, you can use it to open the camera  app. When the camera app is open, you can take   a photo or start and stop a video with a single  press. If you press and hold the button in photo   mode, you can record a QuickCapture video, similar  to the Capture button of the app. But that's not   all. The brilliant thing is that if you swipe  with light pressure up or down on the button or   to the left or right, depending on how you hold  the iPhone, you can change various settings.   As you can see, I change the exposure by swiping  up and down, just like with the Exposure Control   feature. You can also change other settings  with the camera control button. To do this,   lightly press the button twice and a menu will  appear. You can now select the feature you want to   adjust by swiping. In addition to the exposure,  you can change the F-Stop for portrait shots,   the zoom factor, whereby the zoom for all values  between the various cameras leads to a digital   crop and a reduction in image quality. This is  why it is also possible to switch only between   the cameras, which is clearly the better option.  At the bottom you can change the styles and even   adjust the tone of your style. when you change  settings with the camera control button, all other   information disappears from the screen - so you  can better assess the effects on your photo. Apple   calls this Clean Preview - you can deactivate  this feature in the settings of the camera app.   And you can deactivate single functions and  customize the functionality of the button. And the button has another very useful  feature. When you press and hold it,   you activate visual intelligence. Your iPhone  can now recognize plants or animals. You can   find information about a business, such as the  opening hours of a restaurant. You can interact   with text—summarize it or have it translated. Or  you can ask ChatGPT for an analysis of the image,   or you can search Google for similar  images immediately and directly. However,   the function must be activated under Camera  Control and Launch Visual Intelligence. Now let's have a quick look at the editing tools.  If you tap on the small image of your last shot   in the lower left corner, you will see your  last image in full size and can edit it in many   different ways. Today I will only give you a brief  overview and point out a few important things. If   you swipe through your last photos, you can see in  the upper left corner if your photo is a portrait   photo or a live photo. If not, this area remains  blank. This is important because portrait photos   and live photos allow different editing steps.  Generally, the editing menu opens by pressing the   settings button here below. As already explained,  here you can subsequently change or customise the   style used. With the adjust icon you can make  basic adjustments to the photo. You can adjust   the colors, exposure, white balance, saturation,  sharpness and so on. And by clicking the   icon on the right you can rotate, crop or adjust  the aspect ratio of the photo. With Clean up,   you can use AI to remove unwanted objects from an  image. And via the pen icon, you can add markups,   stickers, text, and so on. If your shot is a  portrait, as mentioned before, you can use the   editing tools to change the portrait lighting  and the depth of field, that is, the F-stop. It's time to take a look at a few more important  options in the settings of the camera app. With   preserve settings you can for example set the  camera to remember certain settings. For example,   you always want to use the same photographic  style, the same exposure correction or you always   want to start the app in video mode. Then you can  enable here that the app remembers your settings. The camera of your iPhone can automatically  detect text and you can then copy and edit   this text. You don't even have to take a photo  for that. When your iPhone detects a text, you   can see this by the yellow borders and the small  text icon. If you tap on the text icon, you can   select parts of the detected text, copy it, use  it for a search, translate it or even share it.   This feature can be very useful. If you don't need  it, you can deactivate it here in the settings. Now a few very important settings. Under Formats  you can choose between High Efficiency and Most   Compatible. If High Efficiency is activated,  your iPhone saves the photos in the HEIF format.   This saves a lot of storage space and the  quality is said to be even better than that   of a JPEG file. However, Windows, for example,  cannot automatically handle this format. To use   your photos on Windows without an extension, you  must convert them to JPEG. If you activate “most   compatible” here, the photos will already  be saved in the JPEG format on your iPhone.  The cameras of your iPhone have sensors with 48  megapixels. Nevertheless, to save storage space,   in the default settings the photos are  saved in a resolution of 12 megapixels.   If you often want to view your photos on a  larger screen, you can set a higher default   resolution of 24 megapixels here.  I would definitely recommend this. And you can also save your photos in the full  resolution of 48 megapixels. To do this, activate   ProRAW & Resolution Control. HEIC is used as the  standard format here as well. But be careful -   according to Apple itself, the 48 megapixel  photos have more resolution, but they also   lose dynamic range. So for a better dynamic range  you should better take photos with 24 megapixels. If, on the other hand, you want to edit  your photo heavily and therefore want to   save as much information as  possible in the photo file,   then you should activate Apple Pro Raw. The  photos require considerably more storage space,   but contain more information for editing. To ensure that your iPhone actually takes   a photo with 48 megapixels or in the RAW  format, you must activate this option in   the camera app before taking the photo  by selecting it in the upper left corner. title: OnePlus 15 vs iPhone 17 Pro Max - The Real Flagship!
transcript: So, the stage is set for one of the biggest flagship battles of the year, the OnePlus 15 versus the iPhone 17 Pro Max. Both phones are pushing smartphone technology to its limits, but they do it in very different ways. OnePlus focuses on raw performance and futuristic hardware, while Apple refineses everything to perfection with power, precision, and ecosystem [music] integration. Let's see how these two titans compare across display, performance, cameras, and overall experience. Starting with the display, OnePlus seems ready to dominate the specs race. The OnePlus 15 will feature a 6.78 in LTPO OLED panel with a blazing fast 165 Hz refresh rate, the highest ever on a mainstream flagship. Paired with a 1.5K resolution, it promises smoother visuals and better battery efficiency than traditional 2K screens. Meanwhile, the iPhone 17 Pro Max sticks to Apple's proven 6.9 in super retina XDR display with prootion 120 Hz. It might not sound as flashy on paper, but its color calibration, brightness, and HDR performance make it one of the best panels for media consumption and daily use. Moving to performance, both phones bring nextG processors. The OnePlus 15 is powered by Qualcomm's Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 paired with an upgraded cooling system for sustained gaming performance. Powering it all is a massive 7,300 mAh Glacier battery capable of 120 watts wired and 50 Ws wireless charging. An absolute speed demon when it comes to refueling. The iPhone 17 Pro Max, on the other hand, runs Apple's brand new A19 Pro chip built on a 3 nanometer process. Apple's silicon is still the king of efficiency and realworld performance, delivering smoother multitasking, better thermal control, and superior AI integration. Though, you'll need to live with slower charging speeds compared to OnePlus. Now, let's talk about cameras, where things get [music] intense. The OnePlus 15 packs a triple 50 megapixel camera, including wide, ultra wide, and a telephoto lens, offering up to 3.5 times optical zoom. With new image processing improvements, OnePlus aims to close the gap with Apple in low light and portrait shots. But the iPhone 17 Pro Max still sets the standard with its 48 megapixel triple camera array and a game-changing 8x optical quality zoom lens. Combine that with Apple's unmatched video performance and color accuracy, and the iPhone remains a content creator's [music] favorite. In terms of software, the story remains familiar. Oxygen OS 16 versus iOS 19. OnePlus offers customization, speed, and a near stock Android experience. While Apple delivers unmatched ecosystem harmony, years of updates, and flawless optimization. If freedom and flexibility matter most, OnePlus wins. But if stability, reliability, and seamless device integration are your priority, Apple still holds the crown. title: Păzea Apple și Samsung! NOUL Find X9 Pro, șah la iPhone 17 și Galaxy S25!
transcript: What are the first names that come to mind when you pronounce the word?  In your seats, be ready, phone.  That's me, that's me .  Most likely Apple and Samsung. Correct.  I don't know if anyone would instinctively say Xiaomi or Motorola or Honor or Google Pixel or Oppo.  And several factors contribute to the popularity of the first two names mentioned above : market share, brand, hype around them, the geographical region you are in, along with, of course, the innovations and intrinsic quality of those phones.  But that doesn't mean in any way that other manufacturers don't have equally powerful and well-made models, or sometimes even more capable.  The latter's phones are often braver in bringing radical changes and innovations, such as batteries based on newer technologies, such as silicon- carbon batteries, faster charging, more exotic construction materials, bolder designs, or extremely advanced cameras that shatter any established competition, as is the case with models like the Huawei Pura 80 Ultra with its slide-out telephoto camera and that huge sensor larger than the main ones on any Apple or Samsung or the Oppo Find X8 Ultra with its formidable cameras, but which phone is unfortunately only available in China.  Speaking of which, I really like Oppo's Find X series because it has always come up with some really competent models with an emphasis on cameras.  However, I also have a constant complaint about this Find series, namely that its top dog, the strongest exponent, namely the Ultra model, is always reserved exclusively.  for the Chinese market and it's a shame because it's a real beast.  Maybe with the X9 series we could hope for a change.  I missed the spoiler.  Well, OPPO just launched the 9th series, Find X9, and The Big Boy here is the brand new Find X9 Pro.  And to no one's surprise, you'll see that the model here gives the latest phones from Apple and Samsung a run for their money. [music] The color of the model we received in tests is Silk White, the Find X89 Pro is also available in Titanium Charcoal, but I see from various materials available online that there is also a Velvet Red version which is the most appetizing of them, but which we obviously don't have.  Red is back on phones.  Excellent.  Because I'm tired, like the Greeks, of these tired pastels, as if we were living in a poem by Bacovia.  Dude, leave me alone with beiges, grays, and other pale shades because I'm getting depressed.  And those hipster names that look like they're all taken from a perfume commercial.  Returning to silk white, the minimalist finish of the back can indeed induce a more pronounced feeling of sophistication, but I'm a bit sorry that they gave up the pearlescent back on the X8 Pro series.  The perfectly straight edges here also contribute to a premium look, but they also provide a sturdier grip, meaning you have a better chance of being able to use this phone without a case and still have it survive.  So, I don't think the back of the Find X89 Pro is a turn-off, and that's primarily because in combination with the straight edges, it makes it look awfully similar to the iPhone 16 Pro Max.   It's just that the camera module is placed a little more like Hakana, and the flash has been moved from the camera bump to make room for a Hassle Blood logo, which emphasizes the ongoing collaboration with the famous Swedish medium format camera manufacturer .  The snapkey button found on other Oppo phones on the left side, such as the X8 Ultra, is also present on the X9 Pro.  This can be assigned different functions, including AI Mindspace, that AI-assisted screenshot, silent mode, voice recorder, flashlight, translation, screenshots, my favorite, or you can have the 50th way to open the camera.  And since I mentioned the camera, the quick button for its functions is back, which has also received an upgrade, now being more sensitive.  With a quick double tap, you instantly open the camera to capture the desired frame as quickly as possible, like a cat that will get scared anyway when you quickly take the phone out of your pocket.  Otherwise, the Find X9 Pro is a classic phone.  The volume and power buttons are on the right.  You have a speaker, microphone, SIM tray, and USB C port on the bottom frame.  The secondary speaker is located in the earpiece area, next to another microphone.  And the upper part of the frame is reserved for antennas and an infrared sensor.  I find it very interesting that I see this infrared transmitter or IR blaster returning to most flagships.  It's a minor thing, but it can save you in those moments of maximum laziness when you don't want to get up from the couch or reach for the remote control.  The construction of the Findx 9 Pro is very solid, without compromises, and the water and dust resistance has remained at the same high level imposed by its predecessor, the Findx Pro.  That means it meets the IP6, 68 and 69 standards. So you can also use it in the pool or under hot water jets under pressure.   The Find X9 Pro is also quite heavy, but in the satisfying sense of the word, meaning sturdy.  And at 225 g, it's even a little heavier than the S25 Ultra, but below the iPhone 17 Pro Max.  And now let's move under the hood, because here is one of the big surprises.  The Dimensity 9500 processor is MediaTek's new flagship.  It is manufactured by TSMC P3 Nano and reaches around 10,000 points in Geigand 6, specifically 9908 in multicore and 3430 in single core.  A huge leap compared to the previous Dimensity 9400 on the Find X8 Pro which scored around 8000 points in multicore and around 2700 in single core.  This means that this has comparable performance in synthetic tests to Apple's new A19 Pro with Snapdragon 8 Elite.  So it is in the elite of current top processors, which is no small feat for a Mediat.  I didn't mean to sound like that, but I think you understand what I meant .  The problem occurs, however, under prolonged full load.  I don't know if it's the fault of the sample we received, but the Wildlife Extreme stress test in 3D Mark had some somewhat atypical results, especially for a Mediatach sample, processors that usually don't get too hot.  The thermal stability in the first test I did was 30%, so very low.  Although the first run started from a huge maximum of over 7000 points, 20 cycles later it was barely breathing, and the score of only 2142 points on the last run is minuscule compared to other flagships.  Although the test indicated a temperature increase of only 10°C from 30 to 40, honestly, the temperature felt to the touch seemed much higher, but I'm not 100% convinced that we didn't have a problem with the sample tested.  because the results are suspiciously low, so after long breaks of several hours, I tortured the phone a second and third time in the same stress test.  The second run indicated a more normal, but still weak, score of 48.6% thermal stability index.  Either MediaTek released a processor that was considerably harder to master than the 9400. Or the vapor chamber in our sample, which is 35% larger than previous models, according to Oppo, didn't do its job as it should have .  It's something to watch.  Anyway, of course these are extreme cases, as long as you don't play or work on the phone, stress tests in normal use, including filming, 4K at 120 frames per second, the Find X9 Pro didn't get too hot and didn't show any signs of thermal throttling.  If you also factor in the ultra-fast UFS4.1 storage included, you have the recipe for a phone rocket.  Okay, moving on, the Find X9 Pro's screen appears to be similar to the one on last year's X8 Pro, but if you look closer at the specifications, you also see some subtle changes.  The display is now perfectly flat, and the impression is accentuated by a tiny border of just 1 mm and a bit on all four sides. Accidental presses can still occur, but very rarely, and the phone identified and prevented them well every time.  The fingerprint sensor under the screen has also received an upgrade from optical to ultrasonic, and I want to tell you that you can really feel it because unlocking is substantially faster. Returning to the display, let me give you some specifications because it's beautiful.  It's a 6.78-inch LTPO AMOLED, 2772 by 1272 pixel resolution, an adaptive refresh rate between 1 and 120 Hz and a brightness of 1800 nits in high brightness mode and 3600 in peak.  So more than enough even for use in the strongest summer sun.  Add to this the fact that it can reproduce 100% of the DCI3 color spectrum and is Dolby Vision HDR10 Plus certified, as well as HDR Vivid, and you have the recipe for a top display.  The only thing it's missing to be complete is that anti-reflective coating, but I can live with that.  It is also worth noting that it can reach a nit of brightness for those who are bothered by the screen light in the dark and don't want to get flashbang every time they unlock the phone to see notifications.  Watching multimedia content on this beauty of a panel is a joy and just like its previous version, the display also has a 2160 Hz PVM dimming, which means low flicker and restful eyes, meaning that at low brightness levels, it minimizes the flicker felt by the eyes, thus reducing discomfort during night use.  And I have one last sub-point to mention regarding the screen.  Vehicle motion cues, those little dots that move around the screen in response to the movements of the car you're in.  They are there to help combat motion sickness, meaning you can stare at your phone without feeling sick.  It's good that we have something like that. My advice, however, would be to leave the damn phone on the road and enjoy the scenery, if you know what I mean.  Good.  And now let's move on to the user experience and in a non-conical way I'll start with the ai ul.  But before that, allow me a little quibble that has the potential to become a meme like the one with the algorithm. So it's incredible that there are a few in the comments who actually bit that joke we kept making about the iPhone 17 Pro Max and that color that we repeatedly called the orange zone.  So, be careful, someone says something like this: "Don't you think Apple is looking at you for making the phone in that orange color? It's just a coincidence, really. We really thought they were inspired by us. And now let's talk about AI. God, I'm so glad that OPPO listened to us with the dedicated AI submenu in the settings. For two years we've been saying that there's no place where all the functions available on the phone can be aggregated, so that you can quickly find them all in one place and easily navigate the settings. Everything at hand, nice and correct. There's not much new to mention in the AI ​​chapter, mainly because Oppo already had a vast portfolio of capable AI functions. What else can be improved, I say, is the availability of these functions in Romanian so that they are accessible to our users who are not English speakers. But although for example AI summary does not recognize text in Romanian or AI speak will not read the text aloud to you in our language like a  treasure buried in the depths. If you add that text to Mindspace, the AI will summarize it for you in English, and if you ask it something in Romanian, it can relate it to the saved information to answer you. Sure, you scratch your right ear with your left hand a bit , but it works. The same goes for other tasks related to text. You can bring, for example, copy-paste text into notes, and there it can be summarized in Romanian by Oppo AI itself and not by Gemini. It can be translated or, for example, social media posts can be formulated . Based on that text, AI Studio is also present on duty and, as you well know, from here you can reimagine yourself as a pirate, medieval knight or whatever else your heart desires. There is also AI reimage that will redraw your favorite landscapes in different styles, like Vanangoh, Watercolor and so on. and it is now also improved with the new AI Style Transfer, which will take as a reference point instead of its default templates  Reim Image, a photo suggested by you, thus increasing the range of options. The second new feature is group photo composer, through which you can add different people as part of the group photo from which they are missing. The results are not always what you need, but you already know our recommendation. Repeat until you get the desired result. You still have a limited number of attempts, because it is a system with points, but these are quite easy to achieve. But where has Oppo always excelled? It is in the photo editing part with AI. We already have the classic AI Eras Razer, the function with which you remove photo shadows from vacation photos , AI Enhance Clarity, which helps if you missed the focus in your only photo with a celebrity. And Unblur and remove Reflections, I think you can guess what they do. AI recompose has also kept its place of honor in the photo editor and is a great help for those who are not yet masters of framing. It is not as complex as Google's camera coach on the new Pixels and  this is because it intervenes on already taken photos, but not live with suggestions as happens on Google's implementation. Two other new functions are the AI ​​Perfect Shot also found on Renault 14 and AI Relight. The first will rummage through your gallery to find more images of the people in the targeted photo and will select from those photos several different facial expressions of those people, expressions that you can then import into your frame of interest and will replace the faces of those who have their eyes closed or are dissatisfied with how the photo turned out. This function worked surprisingly well, but it is also a bit creepy in places. And AI Relight tries to restore the lighting of the photo. For example, in the case of a portrait in which sunlight falls from the side and accentuates the shadows on the face, Relight redistributes that light for a more uniform effect. Everything is adjustable and instantly comparable with the original so you can see the difference. And last but not least, the AI ​​function, search is helpful because it finds you not only results from applications or from  net, but also searches through the local files of the phone, through settings, notes, calendar and even through screenshots sent to Mindspace. But all of this can also be unchecked in case you don't want an AI to rummage through your files on your phone. And enough with AI, I'm getting confused. And it was still getting confused when I started studying the cameras. I accidentally said What the [ __ ]? Because the Findx Pro has four cameras while the X9 Pro seems to have given up on one of them. What do you mean the newer phone comes with a downgrade? No way. So I investigated the specification sheet more carefully to see what scheme the guys came up with. The two separate telephoto lenses with 3 and 6x zoom on the X8 Pro have indeed been replaced with a single 3X lens certified by Hassle Blood, but the point is that this time the telephoto camera has a 200 MP sensor. That is, if the sensors on the previous model were 50 megapixels,  if you crop on a larger sensor , you don't get as much. And this way OPPO saves some space inside and can come up with a considerably smaller camera module in terms of surface area. Does this work? It seems so . And they didn't just limit themselves to a larger sensor, they also came up with some software magic. Lumo Imaging Engine is a collection of functions that work in the background. That is, you have some AI denoise or Lightning Snap. That's what that thing is called for capturing moving subjects. And this whole suite uses the processor, the graphics accelerator and that NPU to process the image as quickly as possible, assigning each component the part that it is most efficient at. It is also based on this software that the phone decides depending on the complexity of the photo and the amount of light, but also the resolution of the frame. Most of the top phones on the market, although they have 50, 108 or 200 MP sensors, do not natively take photos at these resolutions. They usually use pixel Beaning,  merging multiple pixels into one, a technique that leads to a final 12 megapixel image. Well, with the help of this Lumo Image Engine, the Find X9 Pro captures images at a resolution of up to 50 megapixels by default. Unfortunately or fortunately, the phone chooses the resolution. This way you will have frames of 12, 25 or 50 megapixels natively, without you having to intervene in any way. Of course, you can still force the photos to come out exactly at 50 or 200 megapixels from that Hazle Blood High Race mode. Returning to the cameras themselves, on the main 50 megapixel with optically stabilized F1.5, the photos come out excellent, both during the day and in low light. In normal shooting mode, the frames have a very good dynamic range and minimal noise in the image. And look, I also had the opportunity to compare the results with those of the previous model, the Find X8 Pro , which is also the everyday phone of my colleague Alex, since  appeared. As you can see, for example in this multicolored cow, at the level of detail we have similar results, but the colors are better on the X9, a contribution of that so-called True Color, which comes to the rescue and which is predictably missing from the Find X8 Pro. I also shot a few examples with Hazle Blood High 50 megapixels and even 200 megapixels exclusively for the 3x lens and you can increase the zoom up to 6x by cropping on the same 200 megapixel sensor. A kind of optical quality zoom like the one from Apple. Obviously the details decrease a little, but the result seems to me above what the competition offers and even above the optical zoom of last year's model . And if you want a longer zoom than 6x, from there on the AI ​​intervenes with over processing to maintain a somewhat good image quality. And last, but not least, would be the ultra wide camera with a 50 megapixel sensor and F2 exposure and autofocus. This camera is  there if you need it and being an ultra wide it will distort a bit on the edges. It is the same Samsung ISOSL sensor found on Alex's X8 Pro, so the pictures will be almost identical. I say almost because in the case of the X9 Pro it will stick its tail out once again, that true color function to adjust the colors. I would also mention the portrait mode where you can play with different filters or types of subject lighting. The detection has been impeccable since last year's Find X8, so I had high expectations anyway , even if the weather was quite gloomy. Selfies have also received a quality boost because we now have a 50 megapixel sensor and autofocus. And it is also worth mentioning, I say, the Hazle Blood Master mode, the result of the collaboration with Hazle Blood, which Oppo is very proud of. The colorization is designed to imitate the unique tones of the famous Huzle Blood X2D camera, but you can also screw something up through the settings. Master mode  It supports both 50- megapixel JPEG and RAW formats, ideal for those who want maximum control in subsequent editing. I've used it a few times and I see it as perfect for walking through European cities full of historic buildings. Filming also goes up to 4K 120 fps. A novelty for the Find series. And the stabilization in this regard is quite good, being optical, but if you want even more, there is also an ultra steady mode, but you still lose some of the image surface, because stabilization is traditionally done through cropping. And we're getting close to the end of this video, I say quite detailed. What else can I say? The battery is huge. With its 7500 mAhh , I reached two full days of intensive use, which seems really great to me. But even more interesting, it seems to me that although the X9 has a bigger battery, the difference in thickness compared to last year's model is only 0.1 mm. And this is the great contribution of new technologies  of silicon carbon-based batteries. Charging is also very fast, at 80 W wired and 50 wireless. Apple, Samsung, are you on the receiving end, can you hear me? To conclude, the Find X9 Pro is a more than capable phone and I would say a significant evolution compared to the model it replaces, which wasn't bad at all. It feels good in the hand and performs excellently. It keeps up with any current flagship, be it Apple or Samsung. That's about what I was saying in the introduction, both in terms of performance and features. The camera system is also special and produces some beastly shots on both the main camera and the new telephoto camera. My only objection is that it looks too much like an iPhone 16 Pro Max, and this seems to make the Find X series lose even more of its charm and uniqueness. Although for others, this could be a plus and the motivation they needed to try something else. Let me give you some  prices. Find X9 Pro is 6500 RON, and the simple X9 around 5000 RON. But both also have launch promotions that include a Watch X Platinum Black watch, a fast charger with Gun technology and a case. And now let's go with the algorithm in the comments and tell us what you think of the new Find X9 Pro? What do you think? It fights with the top models from Apple and Samsung. And that being said, we'll stop here. Don't forget about zona.storm, Storm, our online store, where you can find our custom PCs, but also the equally custom T-shirts of the zone. Subscribe here on the channel. I thank you and I wish you all good luck as always.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max vs S25 Ultra | التليفون اللي هيبدل الكاميرا
transcript: Many people asked me to compare the iPhone 17 Pro Max and the S25 Ultra in professional photography within a professional setup. This means we'll compare their performance here in the office with professional lighting, and we'll also shoot with the phones in log mode to see which phone performs better in a setup like this, and which phone could have a camera for yours or even replace your camera. Believe me, this comparison was unexpected and the results were contrary to what I had in mind. First, to see our baseline, let's start with regular video recording using the standard camera app in a setup like this. Let's see how the results were in the videos you see here. I didn't edit anything, and as you can see, the results were very poor. The contrast was too high, the black points were too high, and the skin tone was distorted on both phones. So, how about the two photos you see?  You saw these two phones; they could produce something good, so I spent a lot of time modifying them to try and get the best result. Let's see how the result turned out. As you can see, both have improved significantly, but the sharpness is still high on both, even though I reduced the sharpness considerably on both phones. Another thing I noticed is that the Samsung image here has more noise, but the detail is better than the iPhone. When you zoom in, you'll find that on the iPhone, my features, my face, and everything around me are blurred, but the Samsung performs better. Now let's talk about shooting in professional modes. Here, I used log shooting from the iPhone's main camera app and also Pro Video recording on the Samsung's main camera app. And before we even started shooting, the interface for professional photography on the Samsung was much better than on the iPhone because there you can control many things.  Many things, like white balance, focus, shutter speed, and more, you can control from the app itself. But these things aren't available on the iPhone. On the iPhone, you can only control your frame rate and also the exposure and brightness of the image. Beyond that, you can't do anything. So, that's the first point to consider regarding Samsung. Now let's look at the difference between them in the image itself. Of course, the images you see here have been edited and the colors adjusted according to the chart I have in front of me. And honestly, I was very impressed with the performance of the Samsung. It produced a very nice image, and perhaps its colors are slightly better than the iPhone's. I tried to adjust the colors of both so that they would be very similar and close to each other, but the skin tone was still slightly better on the Samsung. On the iPhone, it was on the yellow side, and I couldn't correct or improve it any more than that. But if we focus on the details, if I zoom in on my chin...  You'll find the details of my beard are a bit better on the iPhone. It's not as sharp as it used to be, so the details aren't artificially detailed, but they're still nicer. Also, if you look at the black squares in the bar in front of me, you'll see that the noise on the Samsung is higher than on the iPhone. The image on the iPhone is much cleaner. The same applies if we focus on something in the background, even if it's out of focus; the noise on the iPhone is also less. But there are two things to keep in mind. First, the video file on the Samsung was slightly less than 900 MB, while on the iPhone it was more than 8 GB. So, of course, there's a huge difference in size—eight times the size. Second, note that the lighting on both phones is different and changes; it gets darker and brighter. I kept everything still, and nothing changed except for me, for example, moving a little left and right.  However, both lighting modes were constantly changing, so if you're shooting professionally, neither of these would be a practical solution. Now, on the iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max, if you're using another app like Blackmagic Camera or Final Cut Camera, you can shoot Pro Res Log 2 or Pro Res Ro and also use the Open Gear feature. Here, I used Final Cut Camera to shoot Pro Res Lock 2. With this feature, you don't need to connect an external Asus D. The first thing I noticed is that the frame angle is much wider than on the Samsung. I also noticed that the dynamic range on the iPhone is better than on the Samsung, which is evident in the harsh light on my back and the shadows on my face. The details are much better on the iPhone, and there's less noise. Another advantage of this mode is that the lighting on the iPhone is...  It didn't change at all; there was no change in the brightness of the lighting. The strange thing is that the controls on the Final Cut Camera are the same as those on the Pro on Samsung, even though the Pro Video on Samsung has more features, like showing false cherries and zippers. But on the Final Cut Camera, the lighting remained constant, as we mentioned. Another thing that distinguishes the shooting on the Final Cut Camera is that you can shoot at the same frame rate as your camera, not just 30 frames per second, but also 29.97. This is much better if you're using two cameras simultaneously—your camera and your phone—because when there's a cut, there's no difference in the audio. This used to happen to me a lot when I was shooting at 30 frames per second. What also surprised me was that the video file size of the Pro Rise Log on the Final Cut Camera was smaller than the Pro Rise Log on the Samsung.  This is a screenshot from the camera app, as its size here was approximately 6 GB, while as we mentioned in the second video, it was over 8 GB. The difference between the two was less than 10 seconds. Now, let's talk about the big beast, the Pro Rise Ro, with its OpenGate shooting. OpenGate means it shoots with the entire sensor, not just a part of it like all its other phones. This is evident in the frame rate difference between the two; there's a huge difference. It feels like I'm shooting with the ultrawide camera, even though I'm shooting with the regular wide-angle camera. Plus, you have the flexibility to adjust the colors, dynamic range, and white balance as you like. But here, to make things a bit more balanced, I simply corrected the colors, and the image quality is truly exceptional—much higher than Samsung's and even higher than the Pro Rise Log's recording. Keep in mind that I'm not the best at color correction for videos, nor am I the one who adds cinematic color effects.  I think that someone more professional than me, or someone who knows how to adjust colors better, would get a much better result with a feature like this than what I achieved. Also, the video file size is 12 GB and a bit, much larger than the previous ones, even though the video itself is about 15 seconds longer. But now for the most important question: which phone can you use for professional photography, replacing your camera or acting as a sleeve for your camera? I think both will give you excellent performance. Perhaps the only drawback of the Samsung phone that I can mention is that its lighting is inconsistent. I think if you use an app like Black Magic Camera available on Android, this issue will be resolved and won't be a problem. Unfortunately, I couldn't use that app, and in fact, the first test I did was filmed entirely with it, but all the colors were messed up. title: OPPO Find X9 Pro vs. iPhone 17 Pro Max vs. Galaxy S25 Ultra Battery Test
transcript: The iPhone 17 Pro Max recently became the longest lasting iPhone we've ever tested by a big margin. But today, it's going up against what might just be the longest lasting phone, period, in the all-new Oppo Find X9 Pro, which is not only powered by MediaTek's new 3nm Demenity 9500 chip, but it features the biggest battery we have ever tested in a phone at a massive 7,500 MIA. That's roughly 50% bigger than the battery on both the iPhone and the Galaxy S25 Ultra, which gives the OPPO a huge advantage. Now, obviously, the iPhone isn't a slouch. It's powered by Apple's A19 Pro chip. And it benefits from the tight hardware and software integration that Apple is known for. Whether or not that'll be enough for the iPhone to hold its ground, though, well, there's only one way to find out. But before we do, quick disclosure. We partnered with OPPO on the channel, and they did give us this unit for testing. But APO didn't have a say in our methodology, nor did they get to see this video before publishing. So with that out of the way, let's get right into it. All right, we'll kick things off in the phone test where after going on the same 1-hour phone call, the FindX 9 Pro is still reporting 100%. The iPhone is at 99% while the Galaxy drops down to 96. Now, obviously, there's no way that the OPPO didn't use at least some of its battery in that phone test. So, I think we'll get a much better idea of how it's actually doing here in messaging where we have each phone texting back and forth with our automated chatbot where an hour of texting later, this time we do see some drain on the Oppo, although it's only by four points down to 96. While the iPhone and the Galaxy aren't that far behind, each dropping by five points. Here in email, we're not doing anything too intensive. We're just opening up the same set of emails and then scrolling through them. Where, as always, we've calibrated each display to the same 200 nits of brightness. And 60 minutes into it, all three phones do really well. The iPhone and the Oppo actually tie there, which is just impressive on the iPhone's part, given how much smaller its battery is, while the Galaxy only does one point worse. meaning heading into browser, this is still technically anyone's game. Now, this browser test though is definitely more intensive than email was since we're randomly cycling through the same set of websites and we're scrolling through them where we'll actually get to see how each chipset performs where we are talking about three completely different SOC's with MediaTek's Demenity 9500 in Yappo, Apple's own A19 Pro in the iPhone, and Qualcomm's Snapdragon 8 Elite chip in the Galaxy where after an hour the FX9 Pro does the best with only a six points drop. While the iPhone actually takes the biggest hit with a 9point drop. But if we were talking strictly in terms of efficiency and the estimated milliamp usage, the Galaxy actually did the best in that test with it technically using the least amount of its battery capacity. Here in Instagram, we're just scrolling through the home feed like you would in real life, where this test probably stresses the dynamic refresh rates on the phones more so than anything else, where all three phones do roughly the same in terms of the percentage points drops with the iPhone this time being the most efficient in terms of its estimated millia usage. The Apple was technically the least efficient in that test, but of course, because of its 7,500 mAh capacity, it was still able to match the iPhone in terms of the absolute percentage point drop. And because of that capacity, it's likely going to have a major advantage here in standby where typically the phones with bigger capacities tend to do better. Where after 16 hours of just sitting there on idle, that's exactly what we see. There's only a four point drop on a Diapo, a fivepoint drop on the Galaxy, which is actually really good given its battery size, and then a 10point drop on a iPhone. So definitely a big hit to the iPhone's battery. as we move on to YouTube. So, it's worth noting here that in addition to having the screens calibrated, we also calibrate the speakers on each phone to the nearest decel count. That way, we're actually testing the phones and not just some random variables, where after an hour of binge watching some phone buff, the FX9 Pro does the best again, dropping by only six points, while the iPhone drops by seven and the Galaxy by eight. In this gaming test, we have each phone playing Alto's Adventure by continuously tapping on the screen to make sure that we're keeping the touchcreens active, where this time we see a much bigger difference. The FX9 Pro drains by only five points there, compared to a 9-point drop on the iPhone and an 8-point drop on the Galaxy, allowing the Oppo to pull a full 15 points ahead of the other two. But look, we're still relatively early in this test. Each phone still has over 50% battery remaining, and the iPhone specifically has this knack for making comebacks as the test progresses. But at least after an hour of simulated navigation, the trend only continues with the Oppo doing one point better with a fivepoint drain compared to the sixpoint drain on both the iPhone and Galaxy. Here in Spotify, our robots are just listening to music, which, you know, at first glance doesn't seem like the most intensive task out there. But because we have each phone continuously playing back music with their speakers calibrated, it does tend to tax the batteries quite a bit. And one thing worth remembering is this is one of those tasks where software optimization can't really do much to help. Meaning, outside of the speakers themselves, the battery capacity becomes the biggest factor. where 60 minutes later, wow, we only see a fivepoint drop on the BX9 Pro compared to the 8point drain on the iPhone and a 9-point drop on the S25 Ultra. So, for the Oppo, I think that's the least I've seen a phone drain in Spotify given how intensive it is, which makes me super curious as to how it'll do here in Snapchat since this one is the most intensive in the entire lineup with us not only taxing the screen and the camera as we record these clips, but we're also using the microphone, we're using the speakers, and the phones are even using Wi-Fi as we upload each clip to Snap's servers where after an hour of doing this in a loop, oh man, only a 7oint drain on the Oppo compared to the 13 and 17 point drain on the iPhone and Galaxy. So, not just better in terms of the absolute drain for the Oppo there, but also in terms of efficiency, which means for the first time ever, we have a phone going into app cycle with 50% still remaining. I mean, a few years ago, no phone made it this far. So, seeing one doing this well at this stage is insane. Now, this app cycle test is also super intensive with us opening and closing the same set of apps in a loop where after an hour and 50 minutes of doing so, the Galaxy finally gives out. I mean, that's a respectable performance, but the iPhone is still at 9% at this point, while the OPPO is at a whopping 33%. So, just a huge gap in terms of the performance between the Android phones. But I want to see what the difference is going to be with the iPhone where even though the iPhone 17 Pro Max broke Apple's records lasting longer than any iPhone that ever came before it at a full 3 hours and 5 minutes into app cycle, it's still short of what OPPO is doing with it still at a healthy 21%. So there you have it. I mean, that is a huge delta, which just to put it into perspective outside of this intensive app cycle, that 21% is good enough to last you 3 hours and 30 minutes of web browsing or up to 5 hours and 15 minutes of messaging. But at least in Appcycle, it's enough to get the Find X9 Pro the top spot in our rankings, officially beating the Vivo X200 Pro with 13% still left to go, allowing it to not only pass the 4hour mark in app cycle, but officially going for 5 hours and 12 minutes before getting down to its last percent, at which point it enters this ultra low power saving mode that we couldn't disable. So technically at this point the app cycle portion of this test is done since we can't open and close the apps like we normally do. So as a partial solution we had the phone go through the browser test again since you know that was pretty intensive. And we did this for a full 30 minutes and then we switched over to Snap where the FX9 Pro then finally gave out. So you know at least officially its total time is 31 hours and 12 minutes into this test. But if we also count the ultra low power mode, it adds over 30 minutes of extra time, making the winner and the new foam buff style battery test champ by a big margin, the Finded X9 Pro. Anyways, that is it for me in this video. Thank you for watching and as always, I'll see you in the very next episode.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Color Fading Issue – UV Exposure Blamed for “Rose Gold” Effect
transcript: Early adopters of the iPhone 17 Pro and 17 Pro Max are reporting decolorization issues that generally begin around the camera island. This issue eventually spreads throughout the aluminum body, leaving only the matty glass unaffected. Many users believe this color fading is triggered by UV light exposure and sunlight. Some make light of the transformation by noting that a faded cosmic orange becomes a sort of rose gold. The exact extent of the issue remains unknown, but Apple is expected to replace affected units as more cases come to light. For further tech news and updates, don't forget to like, share, and subscribe to the TechVoyoager YouTube

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max vs Galaxy S25 Ultra – LOG Video Test
transcript: Smartphones are becoming very capable devices when it comes to shooting video. Now, if you've ever done any kind of professional video shooting, you've most likely shot in a log format. Shooting [music] in log basically means you're capturing a very flat, low contrast image. But so recently, smartphones [music] have been including the capabilities of capturing log footage. Both the Samsung S25 Ultra and the iPhone 17 Pro Max capture log footage. Samsung log on the S25 Ultra and then Apple log on the iPhone. I will say Apple has really pushed the boundaries in terms of what you can capture with your phone with their ProRes mode. But so I wanted to do just kind kind of a very casual test between these two devices. I'm short on time this week and actually this next month I will be very busy. I'm moving, doing all sorts of things.
